In my case it's for my kid who won't be making many calls nor accessing the internet except on wifi but would love to have Whatsapp active constantly. Reload and forget once a year. 2) Long validity and a cheap long validity data plan so you don't have to reload every month. 3) Long validity only with no data plan. Good for backup phones. Avoid Data PAYU for this or turn off mobile data. 4) Long validity data only plan. Good for tablets. Coming Soon. Notes: - Voice/SMS Rates are off-net. - Voice calls are listed for 1 min calls. Actual charging blocks varies. - Plans and rates change constantly so always double check. - Only plans with voice are listed. Might add data only plans later. text: Tron Validity 365 days with a min reload RM10 Internet: Daily RM1 30MB,Weekly RM5 150MB,Monthly RM10 200MB Unlimited *Note - unlimited @ 32kbps after quota **TnC - unlimited means confined to the amount of data subscribed i.e. 30MB, 150MB, 200MB Rates: 20/min 10/sms Data PAYU: 10sen/MB ***Note - Normally one session of PAYU has 3 connection, thus it is more like 30sen/MB. DIGI standard. Friends & Family: 1st 10 seconds FREE (On net only) On-Net (12 lines) 10 cents / Minute Off-Net (3 lines) 16 cents / Minute Port-In Charges: Top-up RM30 Free admin fee only, otherwise RM10 admin fee and RM9 for a new Tron SIM card. Offers: Seasonable promotion (Topup RM100, free RM25, free 150min on-net). Every few months the promotion changes, but fundamentally they are the same. http://www.tron.com.my/tron-lite-sim/rates/ http://www.tron.com.my/tron-lite-sim/whats-new/ The plan may not be better than some mentioned here. But it offers flexibility. Daily, weekly, monthly plan on top of 365 validity, plus free calls and first 10seconds free if Friends and Family. The plan is superb if you need two of them, one needs monthly plan, the second totally just for receiving calls and making those 10 second calls to the first number. Pay RM100, get RM125 can use one year internet at RM10 monthly (200MB 3G speed +200MB @32kbps). Hope the information helps. As mentioned, there is no one plan better than all the rest in all aspects. Extra: Can talk time transfer with just 50sen total costs. Can totally disable PAYU by writing to TRON to fully disable Internet but then cannot subscribe to any plans and thus no internet in case of emergency. text: I have been on Hotlink Tablet Plan for a few months now. Details as below : Hotlink Tablet Plan: Where to get : Only from Maxis Centers. No dealers carry them. SimPack Price: RM30 - Discount from RM 60. Data :1GB Data.   (No PAYU. No data access once quota is used up). Roaming : NIL. Speed : 4G/3G. Validity : 365 days upon SIM activation (For 1st year even if Data have been used up. There after, follows Top Up validity.). Top Up options once initial 1GB Data used up: RM25 - 1GB - Validity : 60 days. RM45 - 2GB - Validity : 365 days. Calls: NIL. SMS: NIL. MMS: NIL. Every Hotlink Tablet SIM comes with a mobile number but that is just to identify the SIM and account. Since there is no USSD commands via SMS, you will need to download the Hotlink RED app. Hotlink RED app functions: (*App can only function when SIM is installed in Tablet or Cellphone.) 1) Activate SIM for first use. 2) Check Balance of Data. 3) Validity. 4) Top Up. *Note: Wifi must be turn off and must be on Hotlink/Maxis network to use the Hotlink RED app. It is free to use and does not count towards your data. The only minor inconvenience of this plan is Top Up. 1) Top Up via Hotlink RED app : No issues. 2) Top Up at top up centres : There is no specific Top Up for Tablet plan so you have to top up RM 60 for the RM 45 2GB option. The balance will remain with the account as informed by Hotlink. 3) Top Up via Maxis Website : Same issue as no 2). Gadget to use with : 1) Tablet. 2) Dual SIM cellphones. 3) MiFi devices.   - * Cannot access Hotlink RED app with MiFi devices. This plan will work well if you have a Dual SIM phone or a MiFi device. It works as I am using it on my 3G MiFi device without issues. I use the Hotlink Tablet SIM as a back up when my cellphone hotspot is acting up. This post has been edited by extercy : Apr 14 2016, 02:11 PM

text: Hi guys, need your expert advice. I'm currently on an old Maxis plan, RM80 per month + RM50 supplementary line. Now I'm thinking of either switching plans or telco. I'm trying to find a data plan that basically gives me unlimited use like Digi RM68 Smart plan. At the same time I'm trying to keep the total monthly bill including the supp line about RM150 below. So my plan is: Data plan + RM50 monthly principal line +RM30 supp line <= RM150. Digi seems to offer the best data plan but as I'm located in Kuching, Sarawak, I'm not sure if their coverage is good enough. Maxis has the RM58 data plan but it only offers 500mb per month. While I don't normally use my phone to view online videos and such, I'd rather not risk a horror bill. Basically I only require the internet connection for my phone updates, emails and browsing. Not sure about Celcom since I can hardly much find info on their plans. online. A friend told me that they have are using a 384kbps unlimited plan but I can't find any details on this. Can someone enlighten me on this matter?

text: SPAM - SCAM Calls & Messages Awareness Guideliness The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has proposed several measures to clamp down on spam. MCMC issued a discussion papers in which it listed out several proposals to combat the incidence of unsolicitied e-mail and SMS (short message service). The proposals include formulating a definition of spam for regulatory purposes, imposing mandatory measures on service providers, and a multi-tier plan to resolve spam-related complaints and disputes. If the problem recurs or becomes excessive, MCMC suggested that users lodge complaints with service providers, failing which a complaint could be lodged with the Consumer Forum, or with the Content Forum for issues related to the content of the unsolicited messages. As a final recourse, users could complain to MCMC. To combat spam originating from overseas, MCMC proposed efforts to initiate the setting up of regional and “possibly” global collaborative alliances with foreign regulators, service providers, non-government organisations, and related projects and initiatives in other parts of the world. Promoting services via SMS is gaining popularity because of the mobile penetration rate in Malaysia has surpassed 100%. However because of the non-existence of the specific guideline regarding the SMS promotions, the SMS for advertising has become free for all industry. As an association we would like to develop and nurture this new advertising channel so it would not be misused and cast a negative impression from the public. Enclosed is a proposed guideline on the SMS Promotion that should be adhered to by all the CPs. The common and standardised guidelines between TELCO and MCMC to the consumers are: English Version 1. Reply STOP and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP send to 32777 2. Reply STOP ALL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP ALL send to 32777 Bahasa Melayu Version 1. Reply BATAL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL send to 32777 2. Reply BATAL SEMUA and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL SEMUA send to 32777 Additional Information Telco should activate a catch-all SMS that being sent to 6 series. If a recipient reply to the 6 series broadcast, with the word STOP, then all the future SMS promo for all the broadcast should be stopped. ON PROMO - Activate all promotions (chargeable) OFF PROMO - Stop all promotions (non-chargeable) Above commands are also applicable for promotion types of SMS messages. With this method, only one database should be maintained for each telco. All the SMS promo via 6 series will consult this database first before broadcast. Enabling reply to 6 series will make it easier for the recipient to Opt-Out without the need to send to a 3 series shortcode. Note: Above commands are agreed by the commision as FREE OF CHARGE and telecommuncations operator has no authority to charge the consumer for above listed commands. Additional Readings SMS Promotion prohibited to be sent are as follows: » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Suggestive Content A genre of texting, containing either text, images or video that is intended to be sexually arousing and could be interpreted to enticement, and the enjoyment of active attraction, or allurement of the offering content. E.g: "Dapatkan video terhangat pelajar universiti sekarang!" ('the word terhangat' or 'hottest' clearly relates to 'sexiness' rather than a literal meaning itself in this message) False Advertising A genre of texting, describing products or services which turned out not to be the way as it offered. E.g.: "Muat turun 3GP yang lucu sekarang!" (but it turned out to be a video in other format, not 3GP as stated in promo message) Misleading SMS Utilising words such as descriptive terms to increase the perceived value of a product or services offered. E.g: "Langgan sekarang untuk muat turun video dan lagu popular secara percuma" (the word percuma is used to attract subscriber but charges will still be imposed on the subscribers after some period and it doesnt stated clearly in the promo message). Maxis » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Screenshots above are examples of the original SMS sent by Celcom Axiata Berhad (Celcom) to its customers. The following are examples of original SMS sent by Maxis Communications Berhad (Maxis) to its customers FAKE - Maxis » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Number of the sender is composed of 6xxxx 3xxxx or in some cases. To your knowledge, 3xxxx numbers have been designated as an SMS access number for the content of third parties and not directly from the network operator. Number 6xxxx I'm not sure, but almost all the SMS received from 6xxxx are promoting or advertising from third parties. So, if you received an SMS from 6xxxx or 3xxxx number, please be careful and read it carefully. Style and sentence structure used is messy and sometimes 'fragmented'. There is a mixture of Malay and English. Normally the words most often used by service providers is as 'Register free', 'Without the charge list', 'free SMS mobile content', and others. Look, they emphasize the word 'free' or 'bonus' in the SMS as a bait to attract the attention of readers and junior free registration. You have a feeling something is wrong? Remember, in most cases, yes, registration is free and no one would ever be charged! You will be charged as agreed and accepted the specified service. Thus, sentences free registration is merely bait and if you sign up, you AGREE considers them subscribe to them and will be charged a premium for the service itself is not sure! See their tactics? There are also some more despicable, 'disguised' as a network operator! See below screenshot: » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « If You Have Accidentally Subscribed ! Maybe then you are not sure or no knowledge to distinguish between genuine and fake SMS, and you have to register / subscribe. Serves this fool and take credit / phone bill. How to get out of this crisis? Follow the steps below, FIRST: Send command to cancel service the same number you used to register. For example, if you send 'ON section' to the number 30000, and to cancel service, send 'OFF section' or 'STOP section' to the same number (30 000). Your subscription will be canceled upon receipt of the SMS confirmation from the relevant authorities. Conversely, if the command 'OFF' or 'STOP' will not cause any effect (no response) or you forget the name of the service you subscribe to, try to 'STOP ALL' and wait for a response from the parties concerned. In most cases, the command 'STOP ALL' this is effective. If this also does not have any effect, then the next step is necessary. SECOND: Make a call to your network and tell them the problems you face. Your network can help identify to services you subscribe to accidental and provides guidance on how to cancel it. Calls must be made to the Customer Service operator, respectively. Conclusion Many think that network operators have the ability to prevent (block) numbers of external service providers. Ability to block is there, but the operator does not have the power to prevent a number unless directed in writing or direct instructions from the authorities (police, government and others). In addition, there are other users who may use the service and prevent the action would cause the number of users can not use the service. In conclusion, consumers should investigate and carefully read any SMS received from a third party. If you are unsure of the contents of the SMS or its contents very suspicious, ignore, and if able to save SMS as evidence if there is any problem in the future. cleaned_text: SPAM - SCAM Calls & Messages Awareness Guideliness The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has proposed several measures to clamp down on spam. MCMC issued a discussion papers in which it listed out several proposals to combat the incidence of unsolicitied e-mail and SMS (short message service). The proposals include formulating a definition of spam for regulatory purposes, imposing mandatory measures on service providers, and a multi-tier plan to resolve spam-related complaints and disputes. If the problem recurs or becomes excessive, MCMC suggested that users lodge complaints with service providers, failing which a complaint could be lodged with the Consumer Forum, or with the Content Forum for issues related to the content of the unsolicited messages. As a final recourse, users could complain to MCMC. To combat spam originating from overseas, MCMC proposed efforts to initiate the setting up of regional and “possibly” global collaborative alliances with foreign regulators, service providers, non-government organisations, and related projects and initiatives in other parts of the world. Promoting services via SMS is gaining popularity because of the mobile penetration rate in Malaysia has surpassed 100%. However because of the non-existence of the specific guideline regarding the SMS promotions, the SMS for advertising has become free for all industry. As an association we would like to develop and nurture this new advertising channel so it would not be misused and cast a negative impression from the public. Enclosed is a proposed guideline on the SMS Promotion that should be adhered to by all the CPs. The common and standardised guidelines between TELCO and MCMC to the consumers are: English Version 1. Reply STOP and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP send to 3277 2. Reply STOP ALL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. STOP ALL send to 3277 Bahasa Melayu Version 1. Reply BATAL and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL send to 3277 2. Reply BATAL SEMUA and send to (Received Gateway) eg. BATAL SEMUA send to 3277 Additional Information Telco should activate a catch-all SMS that being sent to 6 series. If a recipient reply to the 6 series broadcast, with the word STOP, then all the future SMS promo for all the broadcast should be stopped. ON PROMO - Activate all promotions (chargeable) OFF PROMO - Stop all promotions (non-chargeable) Above commands are also applicable for promotion types of SMS messages. With this method, only one database should be maintained for each telco. All the SMS promo via 6 series will consult this database first before broadcast. Enabling reply to 6 series will make it easier for the recipient to Opt-Out without the need to send to a 3 series shortcode. Note: Above commands are agreed by the commision as FREE OF CHARGE and telecommuncations operator has no authority to charge the consumer for above listed commands. E.g: "Dapatkan video terhangat pelajar universiti sekarang!" ('the word terhangat' or 'hottest' clearly relates to 'sexiness' rather than a literal meaning itself in this message) False Advertising A genre of texting, describing products or services which turned out not to be the way as it offered. E.g.: "Muat turun 3GP yang lucu sekarang!" (but it turned out to be a video in other format, not 3GP as stated in promo message) Misleading SMS Utilising words such as descriptive terms to increase the perceived value of a product or services offered. E.g: "Langgan sekarang untuk muat turun video dan lagu popular secara percuma" (the word percuma is used to attract subscriber but charges will still be imposed on the subscribers after some period and it doesnt stated clearly in the promo message). click again to hide.. « Screenshots above are examples of the original SMS sent by Celcom Axiata Berhad (Celcom) to its customers. The following are examples of original SMS sent by Maxis Communications Berhad (Maxis) to its customers FAKE - Maxis » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide.. « Number of the sender is composed of 6xx 3xx or in some cases. To your knowledge, 3xx numbers have been designated as an SMS access number for the content of third parties and not directly from the network operator. Number 6xx I'm not sure, but almost all the SMS received from 6xx are promoting or advertising from third parties. So, if you received an SMS from 6xx or 3xx number, please be careful and read it carefully. Style and sentence structure used is messy and sometimes 'fragmented'. There is a mixture of Malay and English. Normally the words most often used by service providers is as 'Register free', 'Without the charge list', 'free SMS mobile content', and others. Look, they emphasize the word 'free' or 'bonus' in the SMS as a bait to attract the attention of readers and junior free registration. You have a feeling something is wrong? Remember, in most cases, yes, registration is free and no one would ever be charged! You will be charged as agreed and accepted the specified service. Thus, sentences free registration is merely bait and if you sign up, you AGREE considers them subscribe to them and will be charged a premium for the service itself is not sure! See their tactics? There are also some more despicable, 'disguised' as a network operator! See below screenshot: » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide.. « If You Have Accidentally Subscribed ! Maybe then you are not sure or no knowledge to distinguish between genuine and fake SMS, and you have to register / subscribe. Serves this fool and take credit / phone bill. How to get out of this crisis? Follow the steps below, FIRST: Send command to cancel service the same number you used to register. For example, if you send 'ON section' to the number 300, and to cancel service, send 'OFF section' or 'STOP section' to the same number (30 00). Your subscription will be canceled upon receipt of the SMS confirmation from the relevant authorities. Conversely, if the command 'OFF' or 'STOP' will not cause any effect (no response) or you forget the name of the service you subscribe to, try to 'STOP ALL' and wait for a response from the parties concerned. In most cases, the command 'STOP ALL' this is effective. If this also does not have any effect, then the next step is necessary. SECOND: Make a call to your network and tell them the problems you face. Your network can help identify to services you subscribe to accidental and provides guidance on how to cancel it. Calls must be made to the Customer Service operator, respectively. Conclusion Many think that network operators have the ability to prevent (block) numbers of external service providers. Ability to block is there, but the operator does not have the power to prevent a number unless directed in writing or direct instructions from the authorities (police, government and others). In addition, there are other users who may use the service and prevent the action would cause the number of users can not use the service. In conclusion, consumers should investigate and carefully read any SMS received from a third party. If you are unsure of the contents of the SMS or its contents very suspicious, ignore, and if able to save SMS as evidence if there is any problem in the future. text: Same thing just happened to my wife. She is using a Digi Prepaid & lately we noticed that the credit is running out even when we don't use the phone. Upon checking with Digi, they informed us that we subscribed for a service since 30th of July from a company called Union Vector Alliance Sdn. Bhd. which is a shell company that seems to specialize is scamming. This is the link to a description of that company. http://www.tradeboss.com/default.cgi/actio...mpanyid/770166/ This is the type of message that I regularly receive from them via Digi "60142606757 06-Nov-2011  19.26 (016-my number) FREE DOWNLOAD...unlimited terpanas video clips yg siok. CEPAT!!! Taip on YIRA htr ke 37673 " I've already called DIGI & they told me that they can't do anything about this & asked me to call the scammer at the company's call center. (ya rite, give the scammer confirmation that my phone number is active so they can send me more scam sms). Is there anyone here who knows the people at DIGI who can actually do something about this on their customer's behalf instead of asking the customer to complaint to MCMC instead? Apart from complaining to MCMC, is there any other body that we can complain to? From my viewpoint, DIGI doesn't seem to be interested in stopping this type of scams on their customer as they get to earn money. & from the scammer, as long as the customer is not aware of the charge, they get free RM2~3 a day per customer for doing nothing. The only one who loses is the customer. Regards, Dbassnut

text: Tips - Identifying ORIGINAL & FAKE (3rd party SMS's) ORIGINAL - Celcom » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « ORIGINAL - Maxis » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Screenshots above are examples of the original SMS sent by Celcom Axiata Berhad (Celcom) to its customers. The following are examples of original SMS sent by Maxis Communications Berhad (Maxis) to its customers FAKE - Maxis » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « Number of the sender is composed of 6xxxx 3xxxx or in some cases. To your knowledge, 3xxxx numbers have been designated as an SMS access number for the content of third parties and not directly from the network operator. Number 6xxxx I'm not sure, but almost all the SMS received from 6xxxx are promoting or advertising from third parties. So, if you received an SMS from 6xxxx or 3xxxx number, please be careful and read it carefully. Style and sentence structure used is messy and sometimes 'fragmented'. There is a mixture of Malay and English. Normally the words most often used by service providers is as 'Register free', 'Without the charge list', 'free SMS mobile content', and others. Look, they emphasize the word 'free' or 'bonus' in the SMS as a bait to attract the attention of readers and junior free registration. You have a feeling something is wrong? Remember, in most cases, yes, registration is free and no one would ever be charged! You will be charged as agreed and accepted the specified service. Thus, sentences free registration is merely bait and if you sign up, you AGREE considers them subscribe to them and will be charged a premium for the service itself is not sure! See their tactics? There are also some more despicable, 'disguised' as a network operator! See below screenshot: » Click to show Spoiler - click again to hide... « If You Have Accidentally Subscribed ! Maybe then you are not sure or no knowledge to distinguish between genuine and fake SMS, and you have to register / subscribe. Serves this fool and take credit / phone bill. How to get out of this crisis? Follow the steps below, FIRST: Send command to cancel service the same number you used to register. For example, if you send 'ON section' to the number 30000, and to cancel service, send 'OFF section' or 'STOP section' to the same number (30 000). Your subscription will be canceled upon receipt of the SMS confirmation from the relevant authorities. Conversely, if the command 'OFF' or 'STOP' will not cause any effect (no response) or you forget the name of the service you subscribe to, try to 'STOP ALL' and wait for a response from the parties concerned. In most cases, the command 'STOP ALL' this is effective. If this also does not have any effect, then the next step is necessary. SECOND: Make a call to your network and tell them the problems you face. Your network can help identify to services you subscribe to accidental and provides guidance on how to cancel it. Calls must be made to the Customer Service operator, respectively. Conclusion Many think that network operators have the ability to prevent (block) numbers of external service providers. Ability to block is there, but the operator does not have the power to prevent a number unless directed in writing or direct instructions from the authorities (police, government and others). In addition, there are other users who may use the service and prevent the action would cause the number of users can not use the service. In conclusion, consumers should investigate and carefully read any SMS received from a third party. If you are unsure of the contents of the SMS or its contents very suspicious, ignore, and if able to save SMS as evidence if there is any problem in the future. text: I have been facing the same problem from the M-Mode (33311) . Without noticed that I got charged by this 33311 since last year, every month at least 3 messages until one day I found out that my account balance always insufficient balance. This was very surprising me, almost RM100+ got deducted. I am in overseas, I topped up my mobile for keeping my number. However, I don't have any balance left in my account since last year. This is very terrible!! I tried sending an email to this M-Mode company on 08/11/2012, but no feedback from the company. After That on 23/12/ 2012 I received a reminder message "RM0.00. Subscription Reminder. No Subscription fee. Price: RM3.50/SMS. 15SMS/Month. Auto Renewal on 24/01/2013 unless cancelled. To cancel, send STOP HANTU to 33311." So I cancelled it by sending text STOP HANTU to 33311, but STOP HANTU didn't applied to the center so I sent STOP ALL. Meanwhile, I sent an email again to the M-Mode and vent out my anger in email. On 26/12/2012, I've received an email from the company informed me that they have stopped the service since 08/11/ 2012 and blocked my number from the receiving further broadcast message. However, I couldn't understand why I received the above reminder message (23/12/2012) since have stopped and block every single message. Now 22/01/2013, I received the above reminder message again. Nevertheless, I don't cancel it by sending text, I sent an email to the company again and still waiting for their reply. I'm really fed up and bother me with those SMS, please give me some advice. Should I report this to the MCMC?

text: I am the latest victim from unsolicited SMS from 39599. The third party provider who used the number is SHRAD Computing Sdn Bhd. As all my phone bills are within the monthly fixed charges I do not subscribe to itemized billing in order to save RM5. However, recently I moved to online billing and download my bills to check on my children's phone habit and discovered my son has been billed an average of RM3 monthly for unsolicited SMS. On further checking, I have also been charged a similar amount. I called up my service provider Celcom and was told that they cannot do anything because this is from third party. So I said can I refuse to pay the SMS to which they said then my bill line would be barred as they are only the collecting agent and suggested to send STOP ALL SMS for which I have to pay an additional of RM0.15. The reply I received after sending the SMS was " I have no subscription to be stopped'!!!. So, 1) Celcom with 13 million subscriber do not care if someone scams our money without our knowledge. 2) Even if they scam only 1% of Celcom subscriber they (SHRAD) earn about RM400000 for doing nothing other than sending SMS. 3) Celcom make money either way as those SMS contain MMS message via data usage. 4) Even you complain to SHRAD, Celcom still make money by way of call charges. 5) To stop service which you never subscribed in the first place also earn Celcom free RM0.15 for those SMS. 6) Celcom being like the collecting agent. Like debt collectorlah surely get commission. It looks like very lucrative business and I am only talking about RM3 for 1% customers. Imagine the total money this people collect montly.

text: What type complaint you made? It is funny that I received this message below. They think I am a cowherd who doesnt know what is MMS or third party content. I asked how could the charges incur in the first place when I did not subscribe to them. No answers. The Executive Office of the Communications and Multimedia Content Forum of Malaysia (CMCF) hereby refers to your email dated xxxx with regard to the SMS spams which you have received. For your kind information, the CMCF is an independent self-regulatory body set up via the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA’98) and operates under the auspices of the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (SKMM) to monitor and promote industry self-regulation of content over the electronic networked medium. The CMCF governs content by self-regulation in line with the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Content Code (Content Code) which is a set of industry guidelines on the usage and/or dissemination of content for public consumption. SPAM is the common term for electronic ‘junk mail’ – messages sent to a person’s mobile phone that they have not consented to receive; in order to advertise a business or service, or invite you to sign up a subscription-based service. The content of spam messages varies. Some messages promote products or services, while others attempt to trick users into providing bank account or credit card details. Many spam messages contain offensive or fraudulent material. If you have received any spam messages on your mobile through any short codes, below are the attempts that you may take in order to stop it. Unsubscribed Text Messages Reply to the text message by typing STOP, OUT, END, QUIT or CANCEL to the sender. Subscribed Text Messages If you wish to unsubscribe to a text messaging service which you have subscribed to before, you may reply to the message by typing UNSUBSCRIBE Additional to that, we would like to suggest for you to contact your mobile service provider to seek for their assistance on blocking the sender from being able to send you any SMS in the future. For further information on SMS Spams and how to prevent/stop it, kindly visit the websites below: · http://www.skmm.gov.my/FAQs/Mobile-Content...%28SPAM%29.aspx · http://www.cfm.org.my/files/pdf/cfm-update...ates-100330.pdf As such, the Complaints Bureau treats this matter as closed and extends its sincere thanks to you for submitting the complaint. [SIZE=14] Thank you. On behalf of the Complaints Bureau COMMUNICATIONS AND MULTIMEDIA CONTENT FORUM OF MALAYSIA (CMCF) [SIZE=7]

text: not sure what you guys heard bout MNP. But basically here's the whole concept. MNP allows you to port your number currently the number is tied to( exm. 012 - Maxis, 019 - Celcom and 016 - Digi etc), to any other telco service provider that you choose. So meaning if you are using 0128888000 which is now currently tied to Maxis, you want to change to Digi. In current practice, you cancel your Maxis subscription and register Digi, some even fanatic to get back the same 7 digit, 8888000 of course with 016 prefix instead. MNP allows you to keep 0128888000 but you can subscribe to Digi services instead. it means no number will be associated with any telco, so 012 doesn't mean Maxis, and 016 doesn't mean Digi. No matter what VVVVVVIP number you got, you want to change, just pay the RM10 service charge. Impact: Looks to be calls within own network and other network will be abolished as customers won't know whose number is in which network Timeline: Supposedly implemented last year December 2007, the big three telco systems wasn't ready for deployment. It was delayed, but ministry has set dateline by 2008 to deploy, else penalty. This post has been edited by dreamthief : Mar 25 2008, 09:53 PM

text: MNP may be launched nationwide in October PETALING JAYA: Mobile phone users in the Klang Valley may be able to get a taste of mobile number portability (MNP) by September, but the rest of the country will have to wait till end-October. The original deadline for the implementation of the MNP is mid-August but Malaysian Communications & Multimedia Commission (MCMC) wants to wait to make sure all systems are integrated to avoid any technical glitches when MNP is implemented. “The MCMC wants to ensure that it can monitor and manage a small area before implementing MNP nationwide. It will be easier to manage a smaller area although the concentration of mobile users is the highest in the Klang Valley,” said an industry source. MNP allows mobile users to switch networks while retaining their numbers. The Government first got public and industry feedback on the need to implement MNP in 2004. MNP was initially scheduled to be implemented in 2006. There is no denying that certain players were not ready until recently for fear of losing subscribers in a MNP environment. They also took a while to cooperate because the implementation was complex, said the source. Yet the issues, from porting charges (said to be between RM20 and RM25) to the validation time (anything from six hours to one week), need to be ironed out. In addition, the mobile user needs to be educated on the advantages of MNP so that MNP is a success in this country. The greatest fear of operators is the loss of subscribers and analysts have repeatedly said DiGi.Com Bhd will be the sure winner in an MNP environment. Only time will determine that. MNP may be good and it has been recognised by regulators as a key facilitator for competition. But the question is, with all the monies spent to implement MNP, who would get the lion’s share? Is it the consumer, the operators or the clearing house, Talian Gerak Alih Sdn Bhd, which is a third-party operator? Singapore was the first country to implement number portability in 1997 but it was a huge failure as it adopted a two-number system. MNP was re-launched in Singapore recently. In comparison, it has been a huge success in Hong Kong in 1999 and Australia in 2001. It was reported that in the US about 7.8 million mobile subscribers ported their numbers in the first 12 months of MNP’s introduction there. In Hong Kong 60% of subscribers changed operator and that led to an average price reduction of 60%. The reason for MNP’s success in Australia was because it is a “quick, seamless and low-cost experience.” URL: http://biz.thestar.com.my/news/story.asp?f...80&sec=business

text: Want a fast, extensive plan for mobile in Malaysia? Get our Season Pass: enjoy free calls, free SMS, or free data and share it with your family and friends! 1. Who can subcribe to Season Pass? Any existing subscriber of XOX Prepaid Plus, High Speed Pack, Penang #1758 and UDP will be able to enjoy Season Pass. (including OLD XinXun 3-tiers) 2. How can I subscribe to Season Pass? There are two ways you can subscribe to Season Pass: You can make your subscription purchase via SMS which will be deducted from your current available airtime. You can logon to http://onlinestore.xox.com.my/and make your purchase via Credit Card or Online Banking. 3. How many friends or family can I share my MINUTES, DATA and SMS credits with? Unlimited as long as you, your friends and family members are currently XOX Prepaid subscribers, you will be able to share without any limits or restrictions. RM0.50 will be charge to SENDER for each successful sharing. Multiple items sharing is ALLOWED in single share command SMS and will be only charge RM0.50 for successful sharing. Eg: Share 0103333010 D1 V50 S100 1234. (to share 1GB | 50 Mins | 100 SMS) 4. What is the maximum sharing? Voice - Unlimited SMS - Unlimited Data - 10GB 5. What is the validity period? The validity period follows your current XOX Prepaid Plus subscription. *For new Prepaid Plus user, you’ll need to subscribe Data M in order to utilize the data you purchase from Season Pass Package. 6. Once I have purchased MINUTES or SMS when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin immediately upon successful purchase and subscription of Season Pass and right upon your next voice call or outgoing text message (LOCAL ONLY). NOTE: IDD will be as normal pay-per-use consumption via normal airtime deduction. 7. Once I have purchased Data when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin upon your next your next data usage/consumption eg when you purchase Data Package SP D1 (5GB) it will be stored in your XOX account, it will only be deducted once you have commenced with NEW data activation eg Data D3, Data M XD18, Data M XD38 and Data M XD68. NOTE: Data Promo 1.5GB @ RM29.99 is NOT allowed to consume from Season Pass. Once you activate Data Promo, RM29.99 will deduct from your airtime as per normal behaviour.

Once I have purchased MINUTES or SMS when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin immediately upon successful purchase and subscription of Season Pass and right upon your next voice call or outgoing text message (LOCAL ONLY). NOTE: IDD will be as normal pay-per-use consumption via normal airtime deduction. 7. Once I have purchased Data when will the consumption period begin? The consumption period will begin upon your next your next data usage/consumption eg when you purchase Data Package SP D1 (5GB) it will be stored in your XOX account, it will only be deducted once you have commenced with NEW data activation eg Data D3, Data M XD18, Data M XD38 and Data M XD68. NOTE: Data Promo 1.5GB @ RM29.99 is NOT allowed to consume from Season Pass. Once you activate Data Promo, RM29.99 will deduct from your airtime as per normal behaviour.

text: 5. Mengapa perlu ada Akaun Dedikasi (DA) dan Akaun Utama (MA)? Dengan pelan fleksi 2-dalam-1 yang unik, ONEXOX menawarkan caj kadar murah yang lebih penjimatan pada baki DA dan caj kadar biasa yang berpatutan pada baki MA. Mekanisme caj bermula dengan penolakan pada baki DA (kadar murah) terlebih dahulu; seterusnya menolak baki MA (kadar biasa) apabila kehabisan baki DA. Baki MA juga digunakan untuk melanggan pelan internet mudah alih serta membuat pindahan kredit ke pihak lain; sila pastikan anda mempunyai baki yang mencukupi sebelum meneruskan transaksi tersebut. Ketelusan ONEXOX mengadakan perbezaan kadar pada kedua-dua akaun tersebut adalah bertujuan untuk menawarkan kadar tetap sepanjang masa tanpa perlu membuat apa-apa promosi kadar yang berbeza-beza di masa hadapan. Berkonsepkan belian top up secara pukal, pelanggan-pelanggan ONEXOX yang membuat top up pada denominasi yang besar sekali gus akan menikmati lebih penjimatan pada baki DA. Kami mengalu-alukan agar semua pelanggan ONEXOX menggunakan ruang penjimatan ini bagi mengoptimakan perbelanjaan telekomunikasi harian anda. Rancanglah pembelian top up anda selepas ini! Sila rujuk PELAN & PERKHIDMATAN untuk gambaran lanjut.

text: Since you're new to XOX, the prepaid plan you'll get is OneXOX, so you don't really need to bother about the clause about Prepaid Plus. The Season Pass that you choose to buy will follow the validity of your card, which is 28 months if yours is a new card or you've just MNP-ed over. Note though, for example, if you top up RM100 to buy a Season Pass the validity will be stacked so it'll become 28 months + 100 days > even longer validity! If yours is a Data Season Pass, all you've to do is to subscribe to one of these Mobile Data plans and the data amount will be drawn from your Season Pass. There will not be any deduction from your credit balance. You can find more information of the Season Pass here.

text: OneXOX rates are multilevel. Depends on DA/MA balances ... I can imagine the scenario ... Someone just mnp to onexox, it comes with RM5 credit in MA and starts making a call to an off-net number for 3 minutes and 31 seconds. The MA rate for off-net calls is 12sen/30seconds block. This translates to 8 (30 secs) blocks x 12sen totalling 96 sen. In comparison, the same 3 mins 31 secs call to off-net with DA balance would cost (8 blocks x 9 sen) 72 sen. While an on-net call (with DA balance) for 3 mins 31 secs would be ((4 x 7) + (4 x 8)) = 60 sen. Looking at FnF (limited to 10 numbers) rate, a 3 mins 31 secs call would cost (8 blocks x 6 sen) 48 sen. To be fair, oneXOX MA rate is equivalent to existing prepaid plans from Big 3 telcos of 12sen/30sec block. The Big 3 telcos appears to have standardized the rates at 24sen/minute calls and 12sen per sms. The cheapest prepaid rate to all networks is still Umobile power prepaid at 6sen/30secs block (but coverage may be an issue to some). Second cheapest would be SpeakOUT at 8sen/30secs block. Third would be Umobile older "new" prepaid plan at 10sen/30secs block. All plans mentioned is still available now. Has anyone noticed that Umobile has quietly revised their rates from 5sen/30secs to 6sen/30secs now? ..... Let's see how long can they maintain that position before revising upwards again ..... I am more positively inclined to onexox ever since they start offering extended validity and season pass. It is a good move and I believe that the next progression would be to just consolidate the DA/MA rates into one very competitive calling/sms rates. Forget about 5sen for 1st 10 secs ..... I already tried making under 10 secs calls which fails miserably most of the time.

text: The Season Pass is actually quite easy to understand. Just think about making a big deposit (buy a Season Pass package) into your bank account (your OneXOX prepaid account). When you want to make withdrawals of smaller amounts, just use the same method as anyone who wants to use data - subscribe to the appropriate Mobile Internet/Data pass. The only difference is that you won't have to pay (again) for the (data) withdrawal. I THINK OneXOX is just using an existing platform (as in the Mobile Internet/Data passes here) to work with the Season Pass feature. Pretty straightforward, I feel, otherwise can always ask adiwarna here if you're unsure about anything. If you don't keep an open mind to understand something new, it'll just remain complicated if you choose to feel this way. Up to this point, I feel OneXOX listens to what users hope for. When I first got my SIM card, it's only for the 28-month validity. I thought then that their data packages were more expensive, but they started giving extras after. I was also hoping for Season Pass after looking through the XOX website, and it happened. Then we were wondering what's going to happen after the 28-month validity, and then they started having 6-month and 1-year validity extension. Which other prepaid card makes your data available for as long as your line? Not forgetting also that any subsequent top up you make will extend the validity even further, instead of just overlapping like the others? Or choose whichever prepaid makes you comfortable. This post has been edited by RDoe : Sep 18 2016, 08:55 PM

text:
On the 6th, I requested a 3-line port-out from DG to Brand M. By the 8th, two of the lines received SMS requests and I replied to them. However, one line (under my father’s number) did not receive any SMS to reply.
- 1st call to DG helpline: They told me to wait 2 hours.
- 2nd call to DG helpline: They told me to wait 6 hours.
When I asked why, they said the system showed “pending” but couldn’t give me a clear answer. Instead, they asked me to go to Brand M’s counter.

Of course, my main problem is that no one is helping my father with the DG SMS reply system.(The system like my father age)


The issue is that my father(Old GEN) uses an old 3310 and doesn’t know how to reply to SMS. DG claims to be a 5G provider, but their system still runs on LTE, which causes problems. Honestly, it feels like they’re just treating customers like a ball, passing us around without solving the issue.
Meanwhile, DG kept calling me with cheaper offers—like RM35 per line for unlimited data and calls. (Tip: if you try to port out, they’ll suddenly offer you special deals "i cant promise but your guys can try" haha)

On the 13th, I went back to my hometown to help my father reply to the port-out SMS. On the 14th, I received a message saying I needed to make a HOTbill payment within 2 hours, which I completed on time.
Then came the next problem: I received another message saying the port-out was rejected due to contract issues (my contract still has 1–2 years left). When I called DG helpline, they said it would take 72 hours to investigate.

According to SOP, I now need to reply to the SMS system again. In reality, DG is just holding me back and wasting my time. That’s why—even with cheaper plans—I still want to quit.

Above is my story. Until now, no one has helped me solve it, and I don’t understand why it takes so long to handle such a simple matter. If anyone has faced the same case, please teach me how to settle it and advise me on who I can report to

text:
The most inflexible company on the planet. For context, I am a doctor working in the UK but needing to keep my Malaysian phone number (as tied to my bank accounts). I have been paying 28MYR/month to keep the number active. 
I have found that hotlink will allow you to pay a flat rate of 30MYR per year to keep the number active on a pre-paid sim.
I then proceeded to start porting my number out. This has been one of the most difficult administrative tasks I have ever done. In order to port out, I was made to pay FOUR different bills - each time I was told that that specific payment would clear my balance. 
On top of that, when asking for these payments, you are only given 2 HOURS to fill pay it before you have to restart the entire process. I am a surgeon, what if I was operating?? Am I expected to wait around all day for this SMS and pay it on the spot? Otherwise my “port-out” request is rejected??? 
Horrible scam of a company that has terrible service, is terrible value for money and makes it almost impossible to leave. Would HIGHLY recommend to steer clear if possible.

TLDR - if you are a Malaysian based in the UK, use a hotlink pantas pre-paid sim to maintain your number.  Dont bother with this absolute scam of a company.

text:
I’m leaving this 1-star review to warn anyone considering DIGI Malaysia, especially foreigners, expats, or anyone using a non-Malaysian credit card.

DIGI’s online payment system does NOT work with overseas credit cards, despite what their customer service repeatedly claims.

DIGI states that they accept all major credit cards, including overseas-issued cards. This is simply not true. I tried multiple international credit cards (non-Malaysian banks), and every single payment attempt through the official DIGI payment portal link resulted in an error message. The payment cannot be completed.

What makes this worse is the extremely poor customer service.

Responses take an unreasonably long time, often with huge delays between replies, and each response feels automated and unhelpful. Instead of acknowledging a clear system issue, they just repeat scripted answers like “we accept all credit cards,” even when screenshots of the errors are provided.

In 2026, a major telecom company should not have a broken payment portal or unresponsive support teams that can't even solve the issue at hand.

I strongly do not recommend DIGI if you are:
- Using an overseas credit card
- Living abroad
- An expat or frequent traveler
- Or simply expect functional online payments and responsive customer support.

DIGI needs to urgently fix their payment gateway and seriously improve their customer service response times and technical understanding.

text:
I had one of the worst experiences with CelcomDigi.

First, the company sells used phone numbers that were clearly registered by previous owners on other platforms. This makes it nearly impossible to verify your number on apps or online accounts, wasting hours of time.

When I called customer service for help, they were dishonest and unhelpful, repeatedly trying to blame me or other platforms even after I provided proof that my number was recycled. Instead of offering real support, they told me to pay extra just to receive a simple e-invoice proving I owned the number — despite having already answered all their security questions twice. It felt like they were deliberately adding friction rather than solving the issue.

Their final “solution” was to tell me to visit a physical office, with no guarantee they could fix it — even though all I wanted was a basic ownership confirmation for the number I paid for.

The most alarming part? The day after I activated the number, I received a call from a scammer claiming to be the previous owner — they somehow already knew the number had been reactivated. That raises serious concerns about data security and privacy, especially since the SIM was purchased at the airport where they also took my passport details.

💬 Foreigners, beware.
If you buy a SIM from CelcomDigi, you might end up with a recycled number, constant verification issues, scam calls, and zero accountability from customer support. It’s simply not worth the frustration.

I have never opted for Auto Reload due to my frequent time spent overseas, but I need to keep my number active to pay bills in Malaysia. My Super Longlife feature expired on December 24th, so I reloaded RM50 to renew it for another year.

However, Digi used the RM50 to auto-renew a Rombo 35 plan, which I never agreed to. I contacted Digi via messaging, and they deactivated the Rombo 35 plan but have refused to reimburse the RM50. This feels dishonest. I called Digi, and I was told I would receive a refund, but just a short while ago, I received an auto-reply saying they are unable to process the refund.

What should I do next?”

Today is the 26th, and you're already asking for payment🤭

text:
After nearly nine years as Maxis customers, we recently migrated from postpaid to prepaid. Unfortunately, the migration was clearly not handled properly, because we are now unable to log into the Maxis app at all. That means we cannot manage the account or even perform basic top-ups, which is a serious issue.

My partner visited the Maxis Centre TTDI branch hoping to get assistance, since it is the closest Maxis outlet to our home. The experience was incredibly frustrating.

The staff showed absolutely no willingness to take ownership of the issue. No one tried to contact technical support, no one called a colleague, no one escalated the problem, and no one even seemed interested in finding a solution. The attitude was basically: “We don’t know, go somewhere else.” It honestly felt like every employee was working in isolation with zero communication or teamwork.

What makes this worse is the feeling you get as a long-term customer watching a shop full of employees who appear completely unable — or unwilling — to help. After years of paying for Maxis services, it is extremely disappointing to be treated like an inconvenience instead of a customer needing support.

A customer service centre should exist to solve problems. This branch did not solve anything.

text:
Frankly, I am very disappointed with Maxis Fibre’s service. After 18 months of being your customer, I experienced a disconnected fibre cable which caused a complete internet interruption. Although my call was answered quickly, the follow-up action was extremely slow. I reported the issue on the first day, but no technician was arranged immediately. On the second day, I was told to call again just to schedule the appointment. The technician only came on the third day.

This is unacceptable. An issue like this should be resolved within 24 hours, not dragged on for three days. The delay clearly shows poor coordination and lack of urgency in handling customer problems. Because of this experience, once my contract ended, I decided to terminate the service immediately. I expected better from Maxis.

text:
I am very disappointed with Maxis internet service, especially during weekends. This is the time when most people are at home and actually need to use the internet, yet the connection becomes extremely slow and unstable. Pages take forever to load, videos keep buffering, and online games are almost impossible to play.

It feels like the network cannot handle the number of users during peak hours, which should not be happening with a major service provider. We are paying for a certain speed, but we are definitely not getting what we pay for on weekends.

Maxis needs to improve their network capacity and ensure consistent performance, not just during weekdays but especially during peak times. Reliable internet is not a luxury anymore — it’s a necessity.

text:
I am an end-user of Telco service in Malaysia. I have decided to change from Maxis to CelcomDigi for my Internet fibre service. On 21 Dec 2025, CelcomDigi put up a request to transfer from Maxis to CelcomDigi. However, the application has been pending for few days awaiting approval of transfer from Maxis before CelcomDigi can do the transfer setup at my home. I have been following up with Maxis Service Hotline on this matter and also log in call with their customer service. The careline operators asked me to wait for their technical team's call.
On 27 Dec 2025, the technical call me at 3.30pm and subsequent 2 calls within 5minutes,but I missed their calls. However, I called back without response from them but managed to receive their call on 4.51pm. The customer service officer informed me that the application of transfer has been approved. However, after 6minutes later, he called me again and informed that my application has been rejected due to the previous miss call. He asked me to request CelcomDigi to put up the application again. What is the rediculuos process that Maxis wanted to create trouble to end-user like me.
